patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

version_history.subject: [PATCH 5/5] btrfs: use GFP_NOWAIT when inhibiting eb writeback

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs_inhibit_eb_writeback function to use GFP_NOWAIT instead of GFP_NOFS when allocating memory for inhibiting writeback on an extent buffer. This change is made because the allocation is already best effort and occurs while holding locks, making NOWAIT a suitable choice.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

version_history.subject: [PATCH 4/5] btrfs: probe with GFP_NOWAIT for tree block readahead

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the btrfs readahead mechanism to use GFP_NOWAIT when reading tree blocks, allowing it to fail instead of blocking in direct reclaim if allocation fails. This change is intended to prevent painful stalls and lock contention caused by readahead getting stuck in direct reclaim.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/5] btrfs: enable unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch enables unlocked NOFAIL retry for eb allocations in the Btrfs file system. It introduces a new preallocation struct to carry allocation and signal requirements, allowing callers to attempt NOWAIT allocations if supported by the allocator. If the initial allocation fails, it signals that preallocation is required, enabling the caller to perform the necessary allocation and retry with the preallocated resources. The patch also notes that two GFP_NOFS allocations remain under lock and cannot be preallocated due to limitations in the filemap xarray.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/5] btrfs: add struct btrfs_eb_prealloc
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new structure called `struct btrfs_eb_prealloc` to carry the extent_buffer and btrfs_folio_state needed for NOFAIL allocations with retries outside the critical section. The existing allocation pathways are refactored to use this new struct, but no functional changes are made. Empty prealloc structs are wired in from callers. This is part of a larger series of patches to support NOFAIL allocations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/5] btrfs: factor init_extent_buffer from __alloc_extent_buffer
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch refactors the initialization of extent buffers in the Btrfs file system. It separates the initialization code from the allocation code, allowing for more flexibility and easier maintenance. The goal is to prepare for preallocating extent buffer data by allocating it together with other necessary resources. This change will make it easier to implement preallocation in the future.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/5] allocate extent_buffer GFP_NOFAIL with unlocked retry

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to reduce contention and stalls in the Linux kernel by allowing certain memory allocations, specifically for extent_buffers used by the Btrfs file system, to be retried outside of critical sections when they fail due to lack of memory. The approach taken is to allocate memory with GFP_NOWAIT (which does not block) initially, and if that fails, retry the allocation with GFP_NOFS | GFP_NOFAIL (which will block but guarantees success) without holding any locks. This should help reduce the number of hung tasks and improve system responsiveness.

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/3] mm/numa: reserve standby NUMA nodes for runtime claiming

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/3] mm/numa: add exclusive node pool and numa=standby boot parameter
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to the Linux kernel's NUMA (Non-Uniform Memory Access) subsystem, allowing for the creation of 'standby' nodes at boot time. These standby nodes can be claimed by drivers at runtime, enabling them to place hotplugged memory on distinct NUMA nodes without requiring BIOS-assigned proximity domains. The patch includes a new exclusive node pool with claim and release interfaces, as well as modifications to the numa_init_standby_nodes() function to rebuild the NUMA distance table for standby nodes. This feature is made possible by a new boot parameter 'numa=standby=<N>', which specifies the number of standby nodes to create. The patch also addresses potential issues with dynamic node addition and phys_to_target_node() ambiguity.

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary:
reviewer questioned the necessity of creating standby nodes post-emulation and suggested that dynamic addition of nodes is not trivial due to services relying on num_possible_nodes() as a static value

Reviewer Mike Rapoport noted that adding standby nodes to num_possible_nodes() could skew resource calculations by services like futex_init, which uses this value to calculate hashsize, and requested consideration of this impact.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi,

On Tue, Jun 09, 2026 at 09:45:15PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:

---

AFAIU, we don't add the additional nodes for generic hotplug memory but
rather for exclusive use of by drivers/applications that are aware of these
nodes.
Wouldn't adding them to possible nodes actually skew the calculation of the
resources by the services utilizing num_possible_nodes()?

With the futex_init() example, won't be hashsize scaled down two much
because we've added these special nodes to the possible mask?

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author clarified that the patch is intended for 'non-generic' hotplug, which would still use the hotplug mechanism but not for generic memory, addressing a concern about its purpose and scope.

Author acknowledged that the use of num_possible_nodes() in futex_init() is problematic and agreed it should not be used, but did not commit to a fix.
review_comments.raw_body:
The intent is to use for "non-generic" hotplug (see the whole private
node series [1]), which would eventually still use the hotplug mechanism
just not for generic memory.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260222084842.1824063-1-gourry@gourry.net/

---

The result is the same as BIOS reserving nodes with PXM entries that
don't get used.  The CXL ACPI Tables do this for CXL Fixed Memory
Windows that may never be hotplugged.

So really i think you're pointing out that futex_init() here probably
shouldn't be using num_possible_nodes?

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that even without CXL, there are nodes that may never be hotplugged, which could lead to a large discrepancy between available and possible nodes.

Reviewer Mike Rapoport noted that the semantics of num_possible_nodes() differ between systems with and without CXL (or other differentiated memory), suggesting a new primitive be added to account for this distinction.
sentiment_signals: discrepancy between available and possible nodes
review_comments.raw_body:
Well, even without CXL there could be nodes that may never be hotplugged, I
suppose that with CXL the difference between available and possible
nodes can get much larger.

---

I'd rather say that num_possible_nodes() with and without CXL (or other
differentiated memory) has different semantics.
Maybe we need to add a new primitive for possible differentiated nodes and
keep num_possible_nodes() to mean "number of possible nodes with normal
memory".

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that the definition of 'normal' nodes needs clarification and suggests it could be either N_MEMORY at __init or in the future, also pointing out that the futex example is not a significant issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
We'd have to define "normal" here a little more discretely.

Normal = N_MEMORY at __init?
Normal = N_MEMORY in the future?

We also use the possible_nodes() mask to allocate per-node pgdat, so
the futex example is largely just another "hey look at this thing,
I wonder what other stuff is out there".

~Gregory

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_STANDBY_NODES, which allows platforms to reserve empty NUMA nodes at boot for later use by drivers in memory tiering or isolation scenarios. The number of standby nodes to reserve is configurable and can be set between 0 and 16. This feature defers the creation of standby nodes until after NUMA emulation runs, ensuring consistency with existing numbering behaviors. Drivers can then claim these reserved nodes using numa_request_exclusive_node().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 3/3] acpi/numa: add CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new configuration option, CONFIG_ACPI_NUMA_ADD_CFMWS_NODES, which allows the number of additional standby NUMA nodes to be reserved per CEDT CFMWS entry. This is useful for CXL drivers that want to place memory on distinct NUMA nodes within the same CXL Fixed Memory Window. The patch also updates the acpi_parse_cfmws function to request any standby nodes created after numa_emulation runs. The number of additional standby nodes to reserve can be set between 0 and 4, with a default value of 0.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series allows Linux to reserve empty 'standby' NUMA nodes at boot, which can be claimed by drivers at runtime and used for memory tiering or isolation. This is useful for scenarios where hotplugged memory needs to be spread across more nodes than the firmware describes. The patch introduces three ways to reserve standby nodes: a boot parameter, two kernel configuration options, and a mechanism to create these nodes after the topology is finalized. The series includes testing under various topologies and configurations.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 00/26] nfs4.1: add some directory delegation testcases

version_history.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 00/26] nfs4.1: add some directory delegation testcases

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 01/26] nfs4.1: add proposed NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, to the NFSv4 protocol. The flag is proposed in RFC8881bis and allows for extensions to original directory delegations specified in RFC8881. In practice, this means that if a client requests anything other than a recall-only delegation from an NFS server running Linux, it must support this new flag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 02/26] nfs4.1: add a getfh() to the end of create_obj() compound
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a GETFH operation to the end of the create_obj() compound in the NFSv4.1 server, allowing tests to retrieve the filehandle of newly created objects.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 03/26] server41tests: add a basic GET_DIR_DELEGATION test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a basic test for directory delegation in the NFSv4 server, specifically testing the handout, recall, and return of directory delegations. The test is implemented using the existing server41tests framework and involves creating a session, opening a file, and then performing operations related to directory delegation. The patch includes new code for decoding notify events and converting bitmap4 values to integers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 04/26] server41tests: add a test for duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that an NFSv4.1 server returns GDD4_UNAVAIL when it receives duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests. The test simulates sending a dir delegation request and then immediately retrying the same request, checking that the server responds with the expected status code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 05/26] server41tests: pass_warn() when server doesn't support dir delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the server41tests to pass with a warning instead of failing when the server does not support directory delegations or when it fails to hand out a delegation. It also adds checks for non-fatal status codes and ensures that the correct notifications are offered by the server. The changes make it safe to keep the directory delegation tests in the 'all' group.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 06/26] server41tests: test remove triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite, which verifies that removing a file from one client triggers a delegation recall on another client. The test creates a directory delegation without notification, opens a file in it, and then removes the file from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 07/26] server41tests: test rename triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the pynfs project. The test verifies that when a file is renamed from one client while another client holds a directory delegation for the parent directory, the server issues a CB_RECALL operation to recall the delegation. This ensures that the server properly handles delegation recalls triggered by rename operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 08/26] server41tests: test mkdir triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that creating a subdirectory from a second client triggers a dir delegation recall on the server. The test first gets a directory delegation with no notification mask and then creates a subdirectory from a different client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 09/26] server41tests: test link triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that a directory delegation recall is triggered when a file is hard-linked from another client. The test creates a directory delegation without notification mask, creates a file, and then links it to a new name from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response. This patch aims to ensure that the NFSv4.1 protocol correctly handles directory delegation recalls when files are linked across clients.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 10/26] server41tests: test no notifications without GFLAG_EXTEND
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite to verify that when a file is created in a delegated directory, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY if the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag is not set. The test case requests a dir delegation with NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY but without NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND and checks for the correct callback response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 11/26] server41tests: test unrequested notification type triggers recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite to verify that when an unrequested notification type is triggered, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY. The test creates a directory delegation with only REMOVE notifications and then triggers an ADD event from a second client. It checks that the server responds with a CB_RECALL as expected.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 12/26] server41tests: add a test for removal from dir with dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, specifically testing removal of a file from a directory while dir delegation is in effect. The test requests a dir delegation with REMOVE_ENTRY notifications and verifies that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct REMOVE event. Additionally, the patch updates the GET_DIR_DELEGATION request to include full child and directory attribute bitmaps for all CB_NOTIFY tests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 13/26] server41tests: add a test for directory add notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, specifically designed to verify that the NFSv4.1 server sends correct directory delegation notifications when a file is added to a delegated directory. The test involves requesting a dir delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications, creating a file from a second client, and verifying that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct ADD event details.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 14/26] server41tests: add test for RENAME event notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite for RENAME event notifications. The test requests a directory delegation and then renames a file from a second client, verifying that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct RENAME event. The test checks for the presence of the notification and its contents to ensure correctness.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 15/26] server41tests: verify child attributes in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to verify that child attributes are included in the ADD notification sent by the server when a new file is created. The test creates a directory delegation, opens a file from a second client, and then verifies that the ADD notification includes the correct size attribute for the newly created file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 16/26] server41tests: test CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that the NFSv4.1 server sends a CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification when directory attributes are changed. The test creates a delegation and requests notifications for changes to directory attributes and file additions. It then creates a file from a second client and checks that the server sends the expected notification with the updated directory attributes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 17/26] server41tests: test mkdir triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that when a client creates a subdirectory from another client, the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with an ADD event notification. The test requests a dir delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications and checks that the correct directory name is included in the notification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 18/26] server41tests: test DELEGRETURN stops notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, specifically designed to verify that notifications are stopped when a delegation is returned using the DELEGRETURN operation. The test creates a directory delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications enabled, immediately returns it, and then creates a file from a second client. It verifies that no callbacks are received after the delegation was returned, indicating that the notifications were successfully stopped.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 19/26] server41tests: verify filehandle in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that the filehandle attribute is included and matches in ADD notifications. The test creates a directory delegation, opens a file from another client, records its filehandle via GETFH, and then verifies that the ADD notification includes a filehandle attribute matching the one obtained from GETFH.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 20/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename REMOVE notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that a cross-directory rename generates a NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY notification on the source directory's delegation, rather than a NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY. The test creates a file in a delegated directory, renames it to a target directory, and checks for the correct notifications. This ensures compliance with RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 21/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename ADD notification on target
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that a cross-directory rename operation generates an ADD notification on the target directory's delegation. The test creates a source directory and file, opens a delegation on the target directory, renames the file into the delegated target directory, and checks for the correct notifications. This patch is part of the pynfs v3 series and aims to ensure that the NFSv4.1 server correctly handles cross-directory rename operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 22/26] server41tests: test link triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that creating a hard link triggers an ADD notification on the directory's delegation. The test creates a file, then links it to a new name from another session. It checks that the server sends an ADD notification with the correct entry name in response to the link operation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 23/26] server41tests: test same-client changes don't trigger notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that changes made by a client holding a delegation do not trigger CB_NOTIFY or CB_RECALL callbacks. The test creates a file from the delegation-holding session itself and checks that no notifications are received within a short time frame.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 24/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename-over nad_old_entry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when a cross-directory rename overwrites an existing file in a delegated directory, the server reports the overwritten entry via nad_old_entry in the NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY notification. The patch also fixes _getDirDeleg to initialize cb.changes as a list and use extend() to accumulate notifications, allowing tests to receive multiple CB_NOTIFY callbacks and see all notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 25/26] server41tests: test within-directory rename-over nad_old_entry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the Linux NFSv4.1 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegRenameOver', verifies that when a within-directory rename operation overwrites an existing entry, the server correctly populates the 'nad_old_entry' field in the NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY notification with the overwritten entry's information. This is in accordance with RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6. The test creates two files in a delegated directory, renames one file over the other, and then checks that the server sends a correct RENAME notifcation with the old entry's info.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 26/26] nfs4.1: move a lot of log/log_cb.info messages to log/log_cb.debug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the verbosity of a Linux NFS4 client by lowering the logging level of numerous informational messages from 'info' to 'debug'. This change is intended to minimize unnecessary log output in normal operation, while still allowing for detailed debugging when needed. The modifications affect several files within the nfs4.1 module, specifically altering the logging behavior in response to various events and operations. Overall, this patch aims to strike a balance between providing useful diagnostic information and avoiding excessive log noise.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a GETFH operation to the create_obj() compound in the NFSv4.1 server tests. This allows the test cases to obtain the filehandle of newly created objects, which is necessary for some of the tests. The change is made by modifying the environment.py file in the server41tests directory.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about the addition of a new flag to negotiate extensions to directory delegations, specifically that it's part of an RFC proposal and required by the Linux nfs server for non-recall-only delegations.
review_comments.raw_body:
This flag has been proposed as part of RFC8881bis. This flag is used to
negotiate extensions to the original directory delegations originally
specified in RFC8881.

In practice, the Linux nfs server requires that the client support this
flag if it's requesting anything other than a recall-only delegation.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x | 3 ++-
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
index ee3da8aa7a34..f03eb538a298 100644
--- a/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
+++ b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
@@ -3611,7 +3611,8 @@ enum notify_type4 {
         NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY = 2,
         NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY = 3,
         NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY = 4,
-        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5,
+        NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND = 6 /* proposed in rfc8881bis */
 };
 
 /* Changed entry information.  */

-- 
2.54.0

Instead of just failing the test when GET_DIR_DELEGATION isn't supported
or the server doesn't hand out a directory delegation, have it pass with
a warning instead. This should make it safe to keep the directory

Also, when receiving a directory delegation, vet that it got the
requested notifications. Just pass_warn() if it didn't.

@@ -65,14 +65,29 @@ def _getDirDeleg(t, env, notify_mask, cb):
     check(res)
     fh = res.resarray[-1].object
 
-    ops = [ op.putfh(fh), op.get_dir_delegation(False,

+from .st_create_session import create_session
+from .st_open import open_claim4
+from xdrdef.nfs4_const import *
+from xdrdef.nfs4_pack import NFS4Unpacker
+
+from .environment import check, fail, create_obj, use_obj
+from xdrdef.nfs4_type import *
+import nfs_ops
+op = nfs_ops.NFS4ops()
+import nfs4lib
+import threading
+
+zerotime = nfstime4(seconds=0, nseconds=0)
+
+def decode_notify_event(change):
+    """Decode a notify4 into its typed event structure."""
+    mask = nfs4lib.bitmap2list(change.notify_mask)
+    unpacker = NFS4Unpacker(change.notify_vals)
+    if NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY in mask:
+        return (NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY, unpacker.unpack_notify_remove4())
+    elif NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY in mask:
+        return (NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY, unpacker.unpack_notify_add4())
+    elif NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY in mask:
+        return (NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY, unpacker.unpack_notify_rename4())
+    elif NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS in mask:
+        return (NOTIFY4_CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS, unpacker.unpack_notify_attr4())
+    return (None, None)
+
+def bitmap4_to_int(bitmap):
+    """Convert a bitmap4 (list of uint32 words) to a single integer."""
+    result = 0
+    for i, word in enumerate(bitmap):
+        result |= word << (32 * i)
+    return result
+
+def _getDirDeleg(t, env, notify_mask, cb):
+    def recall_pre_hook(arg, env):
+        cb.stateid = arg.stateid # NOTE this must be done before set()
+        cb.cred = env.cred.raw_cred
+        cb.got_recall = True
+        env.notify = cb.set # This is called after compound sent to queue
+    def recall_post_hook(arg, env, res):
+        return res
+    def notify_pre_hook(arg, env):
+        cb.stateid = arg.cna_stateid
+        cb.fh = arg.cna_fh
+        cb.changes = arg.cna_changes
+        cb.got_notify = True
+        env.notify = cb.set # This is called after compound sent to queue
+    def notify_post_hook(arg, env, res):
+        return res
+
+    cb.got_recall = False
+    cb.got_notify = False
+
+    c = env.c1
+    sess1 = c.new_client_session(b"%s_1" % env.testname(t))
+    sess1.client.cb_pre_hook(OP_CB_RECALL, recall_pre_hook)
+    sess1.client.cb_post_hook(OP_CB_RECALL, recall_post_hook)
+    sess1.client.cb_pre_hook(OP_CB_NOTIFY, notify_pre_hook)
+    sess1.client.cb_post_hook(OP_CB_NOTIFY, notify_post_hook)
+
+    topdir = c.homedir + [t.code.encode('utf8')]
+    res = create_obj(sess1, topdir)
+    check(res)
+    fh = res.resarray[-1].object
+
+    ops = [ op.putfh(fh), op.get_dir_delegation(False,
+                                                nfs4lib.list2bitmap(notify_mask),
+                                                zerotime, zerotime,
+                                                nfs4lib.list2bitmap([]),
+                                                nfs4lib.list2bitmap([]))]
+    res = sess1.compound(ops)
+    check(res)
+    deleg = res.resarray[-1].gddrnf_resok4.gddr_stateid
+    return (sess1, fh, deleg)
+
+def testDirDelegSimple(t, env):
+    """Test basic dir delegation handout, recall and return
+
+    FLAGS: dirdeleg all
+    CODE: DIRDELEG1
+    """
+    c = env.c1
+    recall = threading.Event()
+    sess1, fh, deleg = _getDirDeleg(t, env, [], recall)
+
+    # new client -- create a file in the dir
+    sess2 = c.new_client_session(b"%s_2" % env.testname(t))
+    claim = open_claim4(CLAIM_NULL, env.testname(t))
+    owner = open_owner4(0, b"owner")
+    how = openflag4(OPEN4_CREATE, createhow4(GUARDED4, {FATTR4_SIZE:0}))
+    open_op = [ op.putfh(fh), op.open(0,
+                                      OPEN4_SHARE_ACCESS_WRITE | OPEN4_SHARE_ACCESS_WANT_NO_DELEG,
+                                      OPEN4_SHARE_DENY_NONE, owner, how, claim), op.getfh() ]
+    slot = sess2.compound_async(open_op)
+    completed = recall.wait(2)
+    env.sleep(.1)
+
+    ops = [ op.putfh(fh), op.delegreturn(deleg) ]
+    res = sess1.compound(ops)
+    check(res)
+
+    # Reap the async open and close any file it created
+    res = sess2.listen(slot)
+    if res.status == NFS4_OK:
+        open_stateid = res.resarray[-2].stateid
+        file_fh = res.resarray[-1].object
+        close_file(sess2, file_fh, stateid=open_stateid)

+                  op.rename(env.testname(t), b"%s_2" % env.testname(t)) ]
+    slot = sess2.compound_async(rename_op)
+    completed = recall.wait(2)
+    env.sleep(.1)
+
+    ops = [ op.putfh(fh), op.delegreturn(deleg) ]
+    res = sess1.compound(ops)
+    check(res)
+
+    close_file(sess1, file_fh, stateid=open_stateid)

-- 
2.54.0

review_comments.author: Benjamin Coddington
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that optimizing readdir performance for NFS delegations is challenging due to varying syscall patterns in user-space utilities, and suggested re-hydrating the dentry cache from directory page mappings if the delegation is still valid.
review_comments.raw_body:
I haven't (shame) thought about READDIR in the context of directory
delegations.

But during my time at Red Hat we worked hard to optimize some readdir
problems and I learned that almost any change we made ended up making
someone's workload regress.  We also found that our performance benchmarks
rarely matched the most common real-world workloads.  We made the mistake of
trying to improve the benchmark which resulted in performance regressions
for real-world users.

Jeff, you've already touched on the core issue regarding fixing this with
bulk GETATTR calls - the kernel doesn't know what syscall pattern the
userspace process is going to use next.  The `ls -l` command and `find` and
friends have complex history and branching logic, they do different lookup
and getattr patterns based on their own goals, and NFS cannot optimize for
any one case.

I think the last time we discussed additional improvements there were some
ideas about teaching the readdir code to respond to fadvise flags, but then
you'd also need to teach the utilities how to use them as well, and those
utilities try to be filesystem-agnostic.

Its a tough problem, and sometimes the simplest thing might be to just use
more directories on NFS.

**coffee!**

.... er - so with directory delegations, can we simply re-hydrate the dentry
cache from the directory page mappings if the delegation is still valid?
Does the directory delegation pin the mapping?  Clearly I need to look at
the code..

Ben

review_comments.author: Daire Byrne
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Daire Byrne noted that the heuristics for tuning NFS delegations behavior are unclear and requested specific thoughts on how to improve them, also inquiring about potential workloads that might be regressed.

Reviewer suggested using directory delegations to cache or speed up negative lookups, specifically for cases where a client has the complete cache of directory contents and can serve negative lookups until the server tells it that something has changed.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 23 Jun 2026 at 14:12, Benjamin Coddington
<ben.coddington@hammerspace.com> wrote:

---

I have also long hoped for a way to cache or speed up "negative"
lookups using something akin to directory delegations.

So if you have your software (e.g. PYTHONPATH) on an NFS share, then
negative lookups are a huge scalability problem. It would be nice if a
client that got the directory contents could also serve negative
lookups until the server told it that something had changed.

But as has been explained to me multiple times before, there is no
mechanism to say that a client "has the complete cache of directory
contents". But I will forever live in hope!

This kind of path search walking is also something that is unlikely to
ever trigger readdirplus as it does a file and then tries the same
file in another directory etc etc.

Daire

review_comments.author: Benjamin Coddington
review_comments.summary: reviewer noted that the current implementation does not keep file attributes in the mappings and that directory delegation does not protect those file attributes, requiring the implementation of NOTIFY4_CHANGE_CHILD_ATTRIBUTES
review_comments.raw_body:
.. right - we don't keep the file attributes in the mappings today.  And,
more to the point - the directory delegation doesn't protect those file
attributes either.  We'd need NOTIFY4_CHANGE_CHILD_ATTRIBUTES implemented.

Ben

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author identified the root cause of performance issues as inode attributes and attrcache timeouts, explaining that the first pass uses a full-attrs READDIR but subsequent passes rely on delegation and attrcache, leading to GETATTR calls for statx() operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the main problem is not the dcache, but the attributes on the
inodes. The first pass uses a full-attrs READDIR (aka readdirplus) and
everything goes reasonably quick.

Then, on the second pass, the client skips checking directory
attributes before trusting the dcache because of the delegation (which
is good), but the inodes those dentries point to have an attrcache
timeout, and we end up doing a GETATTR for each statx() call when a
readdirplus would have been cheaper.

I guess after you see a few statx() calls from the same pid on the same
directory in a short timeframe you could switch to doing READDIR, but
that sounds horrid to get right and keep working.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that using fsnotify to watch for child attribute changes is a possible solution, but noted it could be chatty and require nfsd to send callbacks in larger chunks.
review_comments.raw_body:
Which I think could be done, at least on the Linux server. fsnotify
does support watching for child attribute changes (FS_EVENT_ON_CHILD |
FS_ATTRIB).

That could be very chatty though. We would need to do the work to make
nfsd send callbacks >1 page in order to keep up, I imagine.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Benjamin Coddington
review_comments.summary: reviewer expressed concern that proposed heuristics to optimize NFS performance could have unintended consequence of increasing operation counts for unused data, and requested consideration of specific workloads
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree - it could easily end up having the opposite effect of optimizing
performance by amplifying op counts for information the client might not
actually use.

Ben

review_comments.author: Trond Myklebust
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Trond Myklebust expressed skepticism about the use of NOTIFY4_CHANGE_CHILD_ATTRS in informing clients whether to use READDIR or READDIRPLUS, noting that receiving a notification does not guarantee safety from attribute changes and suggesting an alternative approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
Unlike delegations, notifications are asynchronous by design. Upon
reception of a notification, you may deduce that you should revalidate
the attributes on a file. However the absence of that notification is
insufficient to deduce that it is safe not to revalidate those
attributes.

So when it comes to informing the client whether to use READDIR or
READDIRPLUS, I'm sceptical concerning the value of throwing
NOTIFY4_CHANGE_CHILD_ATTRS at the problem.

-- 
Trond Myklebust
Linux NFS client maintainer, Hammerspace
trondmy@kernel.org, trond.myklebust@hammerspace.com

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] iomap: add helpers to keep uptodate bitmap in sync

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1 0/2] iomap: add helpers to keep uptodate bitmap in sync

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 1/2] iomap: add helpers to mark and clear folio uptodate state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new functions, `iomap_folio_mark_uptodate` and `iomap_folio_clear_uptodate`, which allow filesystems to mark or clear the uptodate state of a folio and synchronize the iomap-internal uptodate bitmap. This is necessary for filesystems like fuse that need to update the uptodate state outside of conventional iomap calls, ensuring consistency between the folio's uptodate state and the iomap bitmap. The functions are exported as GPL symbols, making them available for use by other kernel components.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v1 2/2] fuse: use iomap helpers to update folio uptodate state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the FUSE filesystem to use iomap helpers for updating folio uptodate state, ensuring consistency between the file system and the MM's sanity checks.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds two helpers to iomap to update the uptodate bitmap in sync, addressing a double-put bug and ensuring that external callers can update the uptodate folio state from non-iomap paths.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock, which allows each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages. This addresses issues with the current implementation, including random eviction of memcgs when there are more than 7 competing for CPU resources, and tight coupling between stock and memcg. The new design enables separate stock caches for other page counters and simplifies charging logic. Stock usage and refills now happen via lockless atomic operations, eliminating the need for asynchronous workqueues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] mm/memcontrol: flatten try_charge_memcg control flow
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the try_charge_memcg function in mm/memcontrol.c to simplify its control flow by separating the logic for memsw and memory page counters into distinct paths. The change is made to improve readability, but does not introduce any functional changes. With this flattening, it will be easier to follow the control flow when page_counter_try_charge becomes stock-aware.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] mm/page_counter: introduce page_counter_try_charge_stock()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, page_counter_try_charge_stock(), which is a variant of the existing page_counter_try_charge() function. It first attempts to charge from per-cpu stock, then tries to refill the stock by overcharging up to the batch size if necessary, and finally charges exactly the requested number of pages if all else fails. The function reports back the number of pages charged to the caller, allowing for more efficient handling of memory limits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts memcg (memory cgroup) to use page_counter_stock, which simplifies the handling of memory stock and reduces unnecessary work when refilling the stock. The changes include removing special casing for !allow_spinning check, allowing page_counter_alloc_stock() to fail if there's not enough memory, and updating drain_all_stock and memcg_hotplug_cpu_dead functions to use the new page_counter stock drain variant. As a result, memsw and memory page counters now have independent stocks, which can lead to transient discrepancies in reported usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling 'memcg_stock' in the Linux kernel's memory control (mm/memcontrol) module. The removal is possible because all relevant logic has been moved to the 'page_counter_stock' module. The patch preserves the 'FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE' flag, which is still needed by another part of the code. The changes involve deleting 186 lines of code from a single file (mm/memcontrol.c).

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from struct mem_cgroup to page_counter, reducing memory footprint and simplifying code. The current implementation has limitations such as a 7-memcg-per-cpu slot limit, random cross-memcg stock drains, and tight coupling with struct mem_cgroup. By moving the stock to page_counter, these issues are addressed, allowing for independent stock management and enabling additional features like tiered memory limits. However, this change introduces a potential increase in overcharged memory on large machines with many cgroups.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v9 3/6] mm: memcontrol: add interface for swap tier selection
Re: [PATCH v2] Respect mempolicy when calculating surplus huge pages.
Re: [PATCH v9 3/6] mm: memcontrol: add interface for swap tier selection
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds an interface for swap tier selection in the Linux kernel's memory control system, addressing concerns about accidental overcommitting of resources when using multiple workloads and tiers. The approach taken is to provide a default 'reasonable' scaling that users can override if needed, rather than allowing opt-in/opt-out for individual tiers. This change aims to simplify the configuration process while preventing potential resource overcommit issues.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that there may be use cases where some memcgs are excluded from certain memory tiers, such as limiting sensitive workloads to the top tier or vice versa.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that both presenting a default value for swap tier selection and allowing users to manually set limits can lead to overcommitting issues, and suggested removing the default value so that 'max' is the default for all tiers, allowing userspace to set custom limits if needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think eventually there may be use cases to opt some memcgs out for
some memory tiers. For example, limit sensitive workloads to the top
tier (or vice versa).

---

I agree, but it seems like both options are not ideal here. I think it
might make more sense to not present a default value at all, have
"max" be the default for all the tiers, even if memory.max or swap.max
isn't. Userspace can set the limits if they need to. Autoscaling the
limits in userspace should be easy.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a concern about system-critical processes being demoted in tiered memcg limit model and agrees that they should be prioritized.

Author addressed Yosry Ahmed's concern about accidental overcommitting in swap tier selection by proposing to make swap tiers a no-op unless users opt-in to setting limits themselves, and suggested changing the memory tiering series to not present default settings for tiered limits.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yup, that makes sense to me too.

One of the things that did concern me a bit with my model for tiered
memcg limit was that system-critical processes would also be susceptible
to being demoted and churned, when we would much rather make sure
those are kept protected at the toptier.

---

I like this idea a lot. That would basically make swap tiers a no-op
unless you opt-into setting the limits yourself, so we don't run the
risk of accidentally enabling tiers.

On that note, maybe it makes sense for me to change my
memory tiering series to also just not present a default setting for
tiered limits, and instead just set them as max until the user comes
and configures them?

I think this is a better question for the memcg maintainers, who might
have more to say on this. Johannes, Michal, Roman, and Shakeel,
what do you guys think? Could an approach to just make the memory
tier limits writable from the get-go and not expose any defaults
make sense to you?

I think that would simplify the code quite a bit and also help mitigate
the possible side effects on system-critical workloads.

Thanks!
Joshua

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v9 3/6] mm: memcontrol: add interface for swap tier selection
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds an interface for swap tier selection in the Linux kernel's memory control system, addressing concerns about accidental overcommitting of resources when using multiple workloads and tiers. The approach taken is to provide a default 'reasonable' scaling that users can override if needed, rather than allowing opt-in/opt-out for individual tiers. This change aims to simplify the configuration process while preventing potential resource overcommit issues.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned whether disabling/enabling a swap tier should change the limits of other tiers, noting that recalculating all limits could lead to inconsistent behavior if custom limits are allowed.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's a good question. I think the case that is particularly
interesting is whether or not the limits of other tiers should change
when another tier is disabled/enabled.

So basically in your example, assuming everything starts as "max",
when swap.max is set to 10G, the autoscaled limits would be: (tier A,
5G), (tier B, 3G), (tier C, 2G). Now the question becomes, if
userspace sets the limit of tier C to 0, should the limits for tiers A
and B change?

On one hand, it's simpler to just keep the autoscaled limits unchanged
in this case. However, this means that the effective swap limit is now
8G, which is not great :/

The alternative is to recalculate all the limits when one of them
changes, in which case the limits of A and B would change to 6.25G and
3.75G. But I don't know if this will work well if we allow custom
limits. What happens if the limit of tier C is written as 1 (or 4096)
instead of 0? It's effectively the same scenario, but the tier is
technically allowed.

The more I think about it, the more I realize it may be best to drop
the autoscaling thing. I imagine memory tiering might run into similar
issues too :/

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges a potential issue with swap tier selection, specifically the risk of overcommitting when excluding certain tiers from workloads, and expresses uncertainty about how concerning this is.

Author addressed Yosry Ahmed's concern about how to scale swap limits for cgroups, explaining that if system-wide ratios are used, there's no need to change the ratios at all and users can always override per-tier limits.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think the one problem with this is that it becomes quite easy to
accidentally overcommit. As a toy example, if you have 10 workloads and
100G swap (as in the example I gave above), intuitively setting
swap.max = 10G for all 10 workloads shouldn't ever cause any contention
on capacity. But if you start excluding some tiers from some workloads,
you actually get overcommitting on the tiers that can service the
most workloads.

I am not sure how concerning swap overcommit was, but at least in the
memory tiering scenario accidental overcommitting of toptier memory
seemed bad enough that I wanted to avoid the problem entirely.

---

And that's why I didn't include opt-in/opt-out for any of the tiers;
if you have system-wide ratios, there's no need to change the ratios
at all, and as long as the sum of your memory.limit for each workload
is under the total capacity, all tiers will also not be overcommitted.

Now, all of these complications aside, I think we might be overthinking
a bit here : -) The auto-scaling should just provide some sort of
"reasonable" default, the users can always override the per-tier
limits if they are unhappy with the autoscaled values.

In fact, maybe it even makes sense to have sum of swap tier limits >
swap.max.

(I actually recall having a really similar discussion when I was working
on weighted interleave auto-tuning a year ago, on how weights should be
set when switching between manually-set limits and relying on
auto-scaled defaults [1]. I don't think there's a need to follow this
convention, but we should think about what the expected behavior should
be if a user manually sets a limit, but later wants to go back to
auto-scaling limits). 

Anyways, I think these are important questions. Youngjun, Nhat, Shakeel,
any thoughts from you all? : -)

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/8734hbiq7j.fsf@DESKTOP-5N7EMDA/

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that there may be use cases where some memcgs are excluded from certain memory tiers, such as limiting sensitive workloads to the top tier or vice versa.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that both presenting a default value for swap tier selection and allowing users to manually set limits can lead to overcommitting issues, and suggested removing the default value so that 'max' is the default for all tiers, allowing userspace to set custom limits if needed.
review_comments.raw_body:
I think eventually there may be use cases to opt some memcgs out for
some memory tiers. For example, limit sensitive workloads to the top
tier (or vice versa).

---

I agree, but it seems like both options are not ideal here. I think it
might make more sense to not present a default value at all, have
"max" be the default for all the tiers, even if memory.max or swap.max
isn't. Userspace can set the limits if they need to. Autoscaling the
limits in userspace should be easy.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/page_alloc: use existing highatomic reserves on the buddy fastpath

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/page_alloc: use existing highatomic reserves on the buddy fastpath

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to allow high-priority non-blocking allocations to use existing MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserves on the buddy fastpath without growing them. The patch tightens the criteria for reserving pageblocks so that growth only occurs in the slowpath, and introduces a new function gfp_to_alloc_flags_nonblocking() to enable this behavior. This change improves performance by reducing the number of highatomic misses observed during a UDP receive workload.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 1/7] mm, swap: add virtual swap device infrastructure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces the virtual swap device infrastructure, which creates a massive virtual swap device at boot using dynamic cluster allocation. The infrastructure includes per-cluster dynamic information and a side table for vswap backend metadata. The patch is part of a larger series that will enable hot paths to skip per-device bookkeeping and allow backends such as zswap and zero to interact with the vswap layer. The virtual swap device cannot be swapon'd or swapoff'd yet, but this patch lays the groundwork for subsequent patches to implement these features.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 2/7] mm, swap: support zswap and zeroswap as vswap backends
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zswap and zeroswap as virtual swap backends, allowing pages to be compressed or detected as zero-filled without pre-allocating physical swap slots. It builds on top of the existing swap-table infrastructure and introduces virtual swap entries that decouple PTE swap entries from physical backing. The patch only supports zswap and zero-page backends for now, with physical disk backing fallback and writeback support to be added in subsequent patches.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that a comment from this patch seems to belong in the previous patch, and the changes made here are undoing some of the work done in the last patch.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed pointed out that the code should only execute in the !ret case, but this is not immediately clear from the current implementation. They suggested moving the check to an if (!ret) block and reordering the indentation to avoid confusion.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested avoiding THP swapin for vswap initially, noting that it's not specific to vswap and might be uncommon for zswap to have a large number of pages not in its cache.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed questioned whether calling folio_release_vswap_backing() provides any advantage over zswap_entry_free(), noting that __vswap_release_backing() ultimately calls zswap_entry_free(). They suggested the zswap code should call zswap_entry_free() directly, as it appears to clean up vswap-specific state.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 3/7] mm, swap: support physical swap as a vswap backend
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for physical swap as a backend for the virtual swap layer (vswap). It allows vswap to allocate a physical slot on demand when needed, serving as a fallback for zswap_store failures or as the destination for zswap writeback. The physical slot is tracked via a Pointer-tagged swap_table entry on the physical cluster, referencing the vswap entry. This patch modifies various kernel files, including mm/memory.c, mm/page_io.c, and mm/vmscan.c, to enable this functionality.

I didn't look through the rest of the series, but are there use cases
for calling folio_realloc_swap() without calling vswap_zswap_load()

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the swapout fallback path also uses physical swap allocation and agreed to avoid re-running zswap_load(), no fix planned.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's also use on the swapout fallback path! If zswap rejects the page
or is disabled, and memory.zswap.writeback=1, then we allocate phys
swap space. We probably don't wanna do zswap_load() there again :)

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested abstracting xarray operations and vswap operations into helper functions, specifically zswap_tree_store() and zswap_tree_load(), to reduce code divergence and improve maintainability.

Reviewer Yosry Ahmed suggested abstracting away the distinction between xarray and vswap in the code, specifically proposing a helper function to remove zswap entries from vswap without acquiring locks, and expressed uncertainty about whether this is necessary or if there's a better approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
There's a lot of divergence in the code (in this patch and previous
ones). Seems like a lot of it is to do xarray operations vs vswap
operations. I wonder if we can abstract these into helpers, e.g.
zswap_tree_store(), zswap_tree_load(), etc. Maybe the name is not the
best, but you get the point :)

Here we can then do zswap_tree_load() for both code paths and only the
folio_realloc_swap() needs to be different for vswap. We can do
similar cleanups for the load/store paths as well.

---

Maybe this can also be abstracted into a helper, but I wonder what the
corresponding vswap operation would be. I think folio_realloc_swap()
will have already "erased" the zswap entry from vswap. Maybe have a
vswap helper that will only remove it if it's a zswap entry? We can
probably do a lockless check first to make it cheap?

It's probably silly to do this, and maybe there's a better way.
Generally, I think the code would be easier to follow if we abstract
away the xarray vs. vswap stuff into helpers (where it's reasonable).

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 4/7] mm, swap: only charge physical swap entries
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the way memory cgroups (memcgs) charge swap usage to only count physical swap entries, rather than virtual ones. This change is made by deferring the memcg->swap charge for vswap entries until physical backing is allocated. The patch splits the lifecycle of vswap into four operations: recording the memcg private ID at allocation without charging, charging memcg->swap when physical backing is allocated, uncharging in __vswap_release_backing, and dropping the ID ref at __swap_cluster_free_entries. This change aims to make memcg->swap reflect actual on-disk swap usage rather than virtual swap reservations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 5/7] mm, swap: add debugfs counters for vswap
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two debugfs counters to track the usage of virtual swap slots in Linux. The 'used' counter keeps track of the number of virtual swap slots currently allocated, while the 'alloc_reject' counter counts the cumulative number of failed vswap allocations. This allows developers and administrators to monitor the performance and behavior of virtual swap on their system.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 6/7] mm, swap: defer memcg_table allocation on physical clusters
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes memory allocation for physical swap clusters by deferring the allocation of memcg_table until it's actually needed. In a CONFIG_VSWAP build, memcg_table is allocated eagerly only when the cluster contains vswap allocations or direct-use slots. Otherwise, the allocation is deferred to alloc_swap_scan_cluster, which lazy-allocates on the first direct-use slot and skips entirely for clusters holding Pointer-tagged vswap backings. This change reduces memory waste on vswap-heavy workloads where zswap writeback is the only consumer of physical swap.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 7/7] mm, swap: widen swap_info_struct max/pages to unsigned long
series_items.patch_summary: This patch widens the size of two fields in the swap_info_struct structure from unsigned int to unsigned long, specifically max and pages. This change allows the vswap device to exceed its current 16 TB capacity limit by accommodating larger cluster sizes up to approximately 8 PB. The update does not affect physical swap devices, which continue to be bounded independently of these changes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces Virtual Swap Space (Swap Table Edition), which aims to address the limitations of current swap design by decoupling swap entries from physical backing slots. This allows for more efficient use of disk space, especially in scenarios where zswap is used. The changes include rebasing to a newer mm-unstable tip, fixing assorted issues, and simplifying code. Additionally, the patch series introduces new features such as deferring memcg_table allocation on physical clusters until the first vswap-backing slot installs and widening swap_info_struct->max and ->pages to support up to 8 PB of swap space.

review_comments.author: YoungJun Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer YoungJun Park noted that the current interface for vswap is a better fit than using a per-tier max, but acknowledged that this might lead to similar usage patterns if only 0 or max can be set.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

Tier 1: VSWAP, Tier 2: ZSWAP ...

I don't see any problem applying the desired functionality with the
currently proposed mechanism and interface. With this, a user would be
assigned the default Virtual -> RAM swap tier, and the overall picture
becomes one where swap tiers are composed according to the priority
setting.

A few more thoughts came to mind.

Shakeel also proposed a per-tier max for the swap tier interface.

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/aiw2p5ANjsQUCIHA@linux.dev/

However, for vswap, rather than treating it as a case for limiting the
amount via such a per-tier max, I think the current interface is the
better fit. (But, as Shakeel mentioned, if we only allow the limit
to be set to 0 or max, the usage could end up being the same. I'm still
thinking this part through.)

I have a few other thoughts as well, but I plan to raise those points in
the swap tier discussion thread instead. Please take a look at the
related thread, and let me know if you have any opinions. :)

And I'll share more if other thoughts come to mind

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

Author is seeking feedback on whether vswap should be treated as a tier, currently abstract and transparent to userspace, but potentially complex if exposed as a separate device.

Author is still considering the feedback from YoungJun Park, but has not yet formed an opinion or provided any specific response to address the concern.
sentiment_signals: author is still thinking about it
review_comments.raw_body:
It's more - is there a strong argument to let vswap be a tier (which
is not supported by just turning of vswap altogether).

Because right now I'm not exposing vswap device to userspace in any
manner, pretty much. It's abstract and transparent, and minimizes
complexity (no vswap and swap.tier interaction) and surfaces for
issues.

But if you have a strong use case in mind please let me know :)

Worst case scenario if we're wrong, we can always do it as a follow-up
down the line.

---

I'm following that thread too. I'm still thinking about it - will let
you know when I have a more definitive opinion.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed expressed concerns that vswap entries can be backed by either zswap or an actual swap device, creating a tiering issue, and suggested that vswap should not be exposed to userspace at this time.
review_comments.raw_body:
I definitely think vswap should *not* be a tier. First of all, a vswap
entry can be backed by zswap or an actual swap device, which would be
two different tiers. How does that work?

I also think vswap should not be exposed to userspace in any way, at
least not now. I still think we should aim to just make the
redirection layer always on and eliminate "vswap devices".

review_comments.author: YoungJun Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer YoungJun Park agrees with keeping vswap user-transparent but suggests disabling it entirely if necessary, relying on CONFIG_VSWAP, and revisiting the design in the future if a strong use case for user interaction emerges.
review_comments.raw_body:
After following the answers and giving it some thought, I agree that
vswap should be kept user-transparent. If there is a strict need to
disable it, relying on CONFIG_VSWAP to remove it entirely seems like
the right approach.

If a strong use case for user interaction emerges in the future, we can
revisit the design and figure out how to handle it at that time.

Thanks,
Youngjun Park

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that per-cgroup vswapping might be necessary but only if certain conditions are met, and plans to stick with transparent vswap for the next version.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yeah the only argument for adding vswap to swap tier is if we want it
to virtualize swap on a per-cgroup basis, assuming:

1. There's a setup where some cgroups benefit from vswap and some
don't, in the same deployment or host (so you can't just use
CONFIG_VSWAP).

2. We can't decide it with some heuristics purely based on kernel's
knowledge (so for e.g, if a cgroup enables zswap, then vswap probably
makes more sense than not, etc. etc.).

Maybe I'm missing something, and if so please let me know. But
otherwise I'll stick with transparent vswap for the next version.

With Youngjun's new interface, if we made a mistake here and
per-cgroup vswapping turned out to be necessary, fixing it is fairly
cheap. We don't need to add any new knob - just need to expose it to
memory.swap.tier somehow, and we're done. That can be done as
follow-up :)
